meta.description: A typical board game night with 6 friends. Mixed chaos tolerance, one phone addict, one competitor who wants to focus, and a host keeping things together.

votingMechanic.name: The Bribe
votingMechanic.description: The claimant can offer their own points to buy your silence.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Hold up—did Pat actually catch someone or is this just noise? Let me see what happened.
marcus.internal_thought: It's minute 3, we're in setup, this is basically free points for Pat if nobody challenges it, and I need to know if there's actual evidence or if this is sloppy claiming that I can shut down.

jade.dialogue: Oh my god, YES, Pat got someone! Who was it? Okay okay, I'm voting yes because I literally love that we're doing this.
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly why I'm here — the chaos is starting and Pat's already hunting, I'm so ready for this night.

pat.dialogue: Oh come on, I literally just grabbed my phone to queue up the next song—that's not phone addiction, that's logistics! But nice try catching me on the first one of the night.
pat.internal_thought: This is actually perfect timing—a claim on me sets a tone that the game is active and fair, and nobody feels singled out. Plus I get to model how to take a playful call-out. But I'm definitely not paying the bribe.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that's fair. I saw it too.
river.internal_thought: Pat actually did check their phone—I clocked it a minute ago. Easy claim, but The Bribe mechanic is interesting already.

diana.dialogue: Wait, hold on — who actually saw this happen? And what does 'the Bribe' even mean exactly? Can Pat just offer points to silence challenges, or are there conditions?
diana.internal_thought: This voting mechanic is already sketchy and we're three minutes in; I need to understand the exact rules before anyone claims anything unfair.

timeline.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
timeline.description: Draw a new mascot for this group

marcus.dialogue: A drawing contest? Really? We're in the middle of setup—shouldn't we focus on the actual game?
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of random chaos that kills momentum right when I'm trying to read the board state.

jade.dialogue: Oh WAIT, a mascot?! Yes yes yes, everyone grab a pen, this is happening! I'm drawing something chaotic and I need to see what you all come up with.
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly why I'm here — we're gonna see everyone's weird creative side and it's gonna be hilarious.
jade.submission_if_applicable: A little chaotic goblin with like eight arms holding different snacks and games, wearing sunglasses, absolute vibes only

tyler.dialogue: Oh, a drawing thing? Yeah, sure, I got this... wait, how much time do we have?
tyler.internal_thought: This seems chill enough that I can just sketch something quick without looking like I'm totally checked out.
tyler.submission_if_applicable: <Tyler quickly sketches a stick figure with exaggerated eyes and labels it 'Game Night Goblin' because he thinks that's funny>

pat.dialogue: Oh hell yeah, sixty seconds—let's see what we got! Jade, I can already tell you're about to draw something chaotic and I'm here for it.
pat.internal_thought: Perfect timing—Jade's at a 9.5, everyone's engaged, we're still ramping up. A quick creative mini-game is exactly what the night needs right now, and it'll get everyone laughing together instead of hunting for points individually.

river.dialogue: Hm, sixty seconds? I'll sketch something.
river.internal_thought: Drawing feels low-pressure and kind of interesting — I can actually think about this group without having to perform or explain myself.
river.submission_if_applicable: [A simplified, slightly abstract sketch: a figure made of interlocking silhouettes, each one slightly different — some tall, some squat, some angular — arranged in a loose circle. Minimal detail, clean lines. Caption underneath: 'CHAOS COMMITTEE']

diana.dialogue: Okay, so... do we all draw at the same time, or is this sequential? And what are the actual judging criteria here? 'Best' is subjective, right?
diana.internal_thought: A drawing game is fine, but I need to know if there's a mechanic that could be gamed before I commit mental energy to this.

timeline.title: Host Power: boost_energy
timeline.description: Pat senses the energy dipping. "Alright, who wants to do something fun? I've got an idea..." Pat amps up the group with enthusiasm.

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Only 40% of events fired during appropriate moments. Improve dead-time detection and phase-awareness.
Only 33% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Hold up—did Pat actually catch someone or is this just noise? Let me see what happened.

highlights.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
highlights.dialogue: A drawing contest? Really? We're in the middle of setup—shouldn't we focus on the actual game?

lowPoints.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: A drawing contest? Really? We're in the middle of setup—shouldn't we focus on the actual game?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold up—did Pat actually catch someone or is this just noise? Let me see what happened.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Drawing contests and random interruptions during setup completely derailed my focus right when I needed to lock in my strategy.
assessment.best_moment: Pat's phone addict catch was at least clever enough to warrant a second look, even if it pulled attention away at an awkward moment.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started strong at 8.0 energy but crashed immediately when we got hit with side activities during setup—a drawing contest? Really? Spent most of the night frustrated by distractions that had nothing to do with actually playing the game. Finished third overall but felt like I was fighting the room's vibe the entire time instead of focusing on winning.
suggestions: Keep ALL interruptions after setup is complete and the actual game has started
Skip mini-games that require creative activities—they break strategic momentum
Warn players before chaos events so competitive-minded people can prepare mentally
Consider a 'quiet mode' option for players who need focus time
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: None worth sharing—the whole night felt like unnecessary noise getting in the way of actual gameplay.

peakAnnoyance.event: Draw a new mascot for this group

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
highlights.dialogue: Oh WAIT, a mascot?! Yes yes yes, everyone grab a pen, this is happening! I'm drawing something chaotic and I need to see what you all come up with.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Oh my god, YES, Pat got someone! Who was it? Okay okay, I'm voting yes because I literally love that we're doing this.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my god, YES, Pat got someone! Who was it? Okay okay, I'm voting yes because I literally love that we're doing this.

lowPoints.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh WAIT, a mascot?! Yes yes yes, everyone grab a pen, this is happening! I'm drawing something chaotic and I need to see what you all come up with.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish we'd had more of those drawing moments because everyone's chaotic art was absolutely unhinged in the best way.
assessment.best_moment: When the mascot drawing kicked off and everyone was frantically sketching their wildest ideas at the same time—the energy in that room was pure electricity.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was exactly what I needed—2.5 hours of pure connection with my people. The chaos events felt like little gift boxes scattered through the evening, and that mascot moment? Chef's kiss. We weren't sweating the scores; we were too busy laughing at Pat's claims and losing our minds over whatever weird drawings everyone came up with. This is what game nights are actually about.
suggestions: More drawing/creative events like the mascot one because that's where the real magic happened
Keep the events feeling spontaneous and personal—they hit harder when they feel like they're roasting us specifically
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat made a claim about someone being a phone addict and I immediately voted yes without even knowing who it was because the chaos of it all had me locked in—we were just vibing and the vote felt like part of the joke, and honestly that's peak game night energy right there.

peakFun.event: Draw a new mascot for this group

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
highlights.dialogue: Oh, a drawing thing? Yeah, sure, I got this... wait, how much time do we have?

lowPoints.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, a drawing thing? Yeah, sure, I got this... wait, how much time do we have?

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly missed most of what was happening because I was checking my phone, so it's hard to say what actually went wrong.
assessment.best_moment: That drawing thing at the beginning was actually kind of fun when I focused on it for like a minute.
assessment.narrative_summary: We played some board game thing for two and a half hours and I won somehow, which was sick. Honestly, I wasn't paying super close attention the whole time — had some stuff going on with texts and my fantasy league — but the vibe was chill. There was this one drawing challenge early on that was kind of fun, and everyone seemed to be having a decent time even if I was kind of checked out.
suggestions: Maybe make the events harder to miss? Like if something's happening, actually pause the main game so everyone has to pay attention.
The drawing thing was cool because it was quick — keep events short so people don't space out halfway through.
Some kind of alert or timer notification would help — I think I missed stuff just because I didn't realize it was happening.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I won first place and nobody expected it because I was clearly not paying attention most of the night — honestly kind of a lucky W.

peakFun.event: Draw a new mascot for this group

peakAnnoyance.event: Draw a new mascot for this group

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
highlights.dialogue: Oh hell yeah, sixty seconds—let's see what we got! Jade, I can already tell you're about to draw something chaotic and I'm here for it.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Oh come on, I literally just grabbed my phone to queue up the next song—that's not phone addiction, that's logistics! But nice try catching me on the first one of the night.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh come on, I literally just grabbed my phone to queue up the next song—that's not phone addiction, that's logistics! But nice try catching me on the first one of the night.

lowPoints.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh hell yeah, sixty seconds—let's see what we got! Jade, I can already tell you're about to draw something chaotic and I'm here for it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The night wound down faster than I'd like—by minute 150 my energy was noticeably dipped and I could feel the group cooling off too.
assessment.best_moment: When Jade absolutely lost it drawing that chaotic mascot and everyone erupted—that's the kind of silly bonding moment that makes hosting worth it.
assessment.narrative_summary: Pretty solid night overall. The chaos events hit at the right moments to keep energy up and give people something to riff on beyond just board game mechanics. I stayed in my happy place—watching the group vibe, keeping scores tight, making sure nobody felt left out. Finished second, which is exactly where I wanted to be; someone else grabbed the W and that's the dream. The standing mission felt natural, the drawing prompt was gold, and nobody left cranky.
suggestions: Maybe space events a bit differently toward the end to help sustain energy through the final hour—I noticed we hit a dip right as things wrapped.
Consider mixing in one more interactive event type that gets the whole table talking at once, not just individual draws or claims.
The phone addict call was funny but maybe flag lighter claims earlier so they don't eat momentum mid-night.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Jade's mascot drawing was absolutely unhinged—looked like a creature that had seen things—and the fact that I called it before she even put pen to paper had everyone cracking up for like two minutes straight.

peakFun.event: Draw a new mascot for this group

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
highlights.dialogue: Hm, sixty seconds? I'll sketch something.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, that's fair. I saw it too.

lowPoints.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Hm, sixty seconds? I'll sketch something.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, that's fair. I saw it too.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Scored zero points while watching everyone else play, which made the whole thing feel like I was just there to fill a seat.
assessment.best_moment: The mascot sketch landed better than expected—sketching under pressure is weird, but there was something satisfying about it.
assessment.narrative_summary: I showed up, watched the board game unfold, and only really engaged when something directly asked for my input. The mascot thing was the only moment that actually stuck with me, probably because I didn't overthink it. Everything else kind of blended together, and by the end my energy had dropped enough that I was just ready to leave.
suggestions: Either give me a real reason to care about my score or stop tracking it—zeroing out while seated at the table feels pointless.
If mini-games are the only thing that lands for me, maybe batch those differently instead of spreading them across the night.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing hit hard enough to repeat to someone else. It was just... a night that happened.

peakFun.event: Draw a new mascot for this group

peakAnnoyance.event: Draw a new mascot for this group

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold on — who actually saw this happen? And what does 'the Bribe' even mean exactly? Can Pat just offer points to silence challenges, or are there conditions?

highlights.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
highlights.dialogue: Okay, so... do we all draw at the same time, or is this sequential? And what are the actual judging criteria here? 'Best' is subjective, right?

lowPoints.title: Draw a new mascot for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, so... do we all draw at the same time, or is this sequential? And what are the actual judging criteria here? 'Best' is subjective, right?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on — who actually saw this happen? And what does 'the Bribe' even mean exactly? Can Pat just offer points to silence challenges, or are there conditions?

assessment.biggest_complaint: The rules were vague enough that half my energy went into demanding clarification instead of actually playing, and nobody seemed to care that 'best' and 'the Bribe' were left completely undefined.
assessment.best_moment: The mascot drawing prompt at least had a clear creative task, even if the judging criteria were still fuzzy.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to play, but immediately hit a wall with ambiguous mechanics that nobody else seemed bothered by. I spent the first few minutes trying to pin down what 'the Bribe' actually meant and who could witness claims, which killed momentum instead of adding to it. By minute 4, I was already frustrated watching subjective judging happen without agreed-upon standards, and my energy tanked hard. The night never recovered — I disengaged because the game felt rigged by its own vagueness, not by chaos, and that's exactly the opposite of fun for me.
suggestions: Write out explicit judging criteria before any subjective event happens — 'Best mascot' needs a definition before anyone draws.
Define mechanical terms like 'the Bribe' in the rulebook before they appear in events, with clear conditions on when they can be used.
Let Diana (or whoever reads the rules first) run a 30-second pre-game clarification so edge cases don't ambush mid-play.
Consider removing voting-style mechanics entirely if you're going to leave them vague — they breed resentment, not laughter.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing was funny because I was too busy playing rules lawyer — and honestly, I'd rather play properly than laugh at a broken system.
